serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Ken Mapo Soup with Egg as served at a Japanese ramen restaurant, approximately 450 g including broth, tofu, ground meat, and one whole egg.

nutrient_source.title: Mapo tofu soup, estimated from USDA FoodData Central component ingredients (tofu, ground pork, egg, broth, doubanjiang) and USDA SR Legacy reference values for Japanese-style spicy soup
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Ken Mapo Soup with Egg exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values estimated by weighted ingredient composition: dashi/chicken broth (~55% by weight), silken tofu (~20%), ground pork (~10%), egg (~8%), doubanjiang/miso/seasonings (~5%), chilli oil/sesame oil (~2%). Per-100g values derived from USDA SR Legacy entries for each component. Energy cross-checked against USDA Foundation Foods for similar spicy tofu soup dishes. Values represent a plausible central estimate for a restaurant-prepared Japanese mapo soup.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of tofu-based and mixed protein-broth soups; analogous estimate based on mapo tofu and similar spicy tofu soup entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ken Mapo Soup with Egg. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous entries in the University of Sydney GI Database for tofu-based dishes (GI ~15–40) and mixed protein-broth soups. The dominant carbohydrate sources are tofu and miso paste, both of which have low GI profiles. Egg and pork contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 17.1) / 100 ≈ 6.0, rounded to 6.5 accounting for serving size of 450 g (3.8 g carb per 100 g × 4.5 = 17.1 g carb per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from doubanjiang, miso paste, soy sauce, and broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Ken Mapo Soup with Egg due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g (approximately 2,160 mg per 450 g serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced-sodium broth and reduced seasoning is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by tofu, egg, and protein-rich broth with minimal refined carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ken Mapo Soup with Egg has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 6.5 per serving, making it a relatively suitable option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though the high sodium content and potential for added sugars in the spicy paste warrant attention to overall meal composition.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from ground pork and egg yolk combined with high sodium from fermented pastes and broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Ken Mapo Soup with Egg in moderation, as the combination of approximately 1.3 g saturated fat per 100 g and high sodium (~480 mg per 100 g) may contribute to cardiovascular risk if consumed frequently; opting for leaner protein substitutes and reduced-sodium preparation is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ken Mapo Soup with Egg is a Japanese restaurant-style spicy soup dish inspired by the classic Sichuan Chinese mapo tofu preparation, adapted for Japanese palates and served as a standalone soup or as part of a ramen-style meal. The dish features a rich, deeply flavoured broth built from dashi or chicken stock, seasoned with doubanjiang (spicy fermented bean paste), miso paste, soy sauce, garlic, and ginger, giving it a characteristic numbing heat and umami depth. Silken or soft tofu is simmered in the broth alongside ground pork or chicken, and a whole egg — either soft-boiled, poached, or stirred in — is added to provide richness and additional protein. The soup is finished with sesame oil, chilli oil, and sliced spring onions. The texture is silky and warming, with the tofu absorbing the spiced broth and the egg adding a creamy contrast. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (approximately 72 kcal per 100 g), high in protein relative to its carbohydrate content, and low in glycemic index, making it broadly suitable for blood sugar management. However, the sodium content is notably high due to the fermented pastes and seasoned broth, which is a clinical concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is served at Japanese ramen restaurants in Singapore, including Ramen Matsuri, and is priced at approximately SGD 18.80 per bowl.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Ken Mapo Soup with Egg was found in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central exact match, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from weighted ingredient composition using USDA SR Legacy component data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on tofu-based soup entries. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ingredient composition (USDA SR Legacy) as no exact approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. Values should be treated as plausible central estimates and updated if an exact source entry becomes available. 2. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on tofu-based soup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement exists for this dish. 3. Sodium content is high due to doubanjiang, miso, and seasoned broth; the per-100g value of 480 mg is a central estimate and may vary significantly by restaurant preparation. 4. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as naturally occurring trans fats in pork are negligible and no industrial trans fats are expected in this preparation; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy component analysis. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.6), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.8), potassium_mg (145.0), calcium_mg (55.0), iron_mg (1.0), cholesterol_mg (62.0), vitamin_c_mg (1.5), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (28.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.06), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.09), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (1.4), vitamin_b6_mg (0.08), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.3), folate_mcg_dfe (18.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.5), vitamin_e_mg (0.6), phosphorus_mg (88.0), magnesium_mg (18.0), zinc_mg (0.7), selenium_mcg (8.5), water_g (84.5) are all estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for the constituent ingredients; no sentinel -1 values are used as reasonable estimates are available from approved sources. 6. The cuisine is classified as Japanese per the input record and the serving restaurant (Ramen Matsuri); the dish is a Japanese adaptation of Sichuan mapo tofu. 7. Halal status is not confirmed as doubanjiang and pork are typically present; is_halal is set to false. 8. Price preserved from input: SGD 18.80 (single restaurant, Ramen Matsuri, 7 North Canal Road, Singapore).
